narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings—pouring your heart into law school dreams only to have them paused like this feels deeply unfair, and I'm here to walk you through why our prediction model saw your path to the bar exam the way it did, based on the details you shared. At the top of what stood out was your Year 3 decile of 2, which was the strongest signal pulling toward a higher chance of not passing the bar—it's like the model saw that lower ranking in your later school performance as the biggest hurdle, reflecting a tougher climb in a demanding program. Right behind that was your Year 1 decile of 2, reinforcing that early academic placement and suggesting a consistent pattern that weighed heavily against success. Your LSAT score of 35 added to this picture, standing out as another key factor tipping the scales toward failure in the model's view. Even your middle family income bracket played a role, as it nudged the prediction downward compared to higher supports that often help students push through. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.2, while solid, didn't lift things enough to counterbalance, joining the mix toward that 83.8% failure probability. Being a white female full-time student wrapped into this too—each of these traits, in combination, painted a profile where the model foresaw real struggles ahead on the bar. It's not about your worth or potential; it's this specific blend, with those low deciles leading the charge, that made the odds look steep. You've got strengths we didn't even touch on here, and maybe focusing on boosting those later-year grades or LSAT prep could rewrite this story next time. This isn't the end—it's a data-driven nudge to pivot and build even stronger. Hang in there; your journey's just taking a detour. With empathy, Your Admissions Advisor
